straight_beat.name: Clyde Orendorff
straight_beat.column:
The Rangers lost yesterday, 8-7, to the Dodgers. That’s the headline. But the real story? The bullpen’s unraveling. Again.  

You know the script: Leal gets rocked in the seventh, Adames can’t close, and the bullpen’s collapse feels like a inevitability. The Rangers have a 7-6 record, but the math isn’t adding up. They’re 1.5 games behind in the AL West, yet the bullpen’s ERA is worse than the team’s overall ERA. How does that happen? It’s not like the starters are lighting it up. They’ve been solid, maybe even decent, but the real damage comes in the late innings.  

Take yesterday’s game. Adames was throwing strikes, working out of jams, and then—boom—Leal walks three batters in the seventh. The Dodgers push across three runs, and suddenly, the game’s lost. It’s not just the numbers. It’s the rhythm. The bullpen’s been a ticking time bomb all season. You can’t build a playoff race on a pitching staff that can’t hold a lead.  

But here’s the thing: The Rangers’ offense is still there. They’ve scored enough runs to win most games. Seager doubled twice, Eovaldi pitched into the seventh, and Texas beat the Astros 5-2 at Globe Life Field. That’s the kind of performance that makes you think, “This team

optimist.name: Lacey-Jean Marcum
optimist.column:
The Rangers lost yesterday, but let’s not pretend that loss was anything more than a blip on the radar. A four-run rally in the seventh inning? That’s not a failure—it’s a reminder that this team still has gas in the tank. The Dodgers won, sure, but you know what? They’re the team with the best record in the league, and we’re still within a game of them. That’s not a consolation prize; that’s a warning shot across the bow.  

Let’s talk about the pitching staff for a sec. Framber Valdez? He’s been a machine all season. And then there’s the bullpen—those guys have been lights out, especially in high-leverage situations. The fact that we’re still in this race despite a few shaky starts says everything about the depth of this roster. You don’t build a playoff team by accident. You lock up the stars, y’all. And we’ve got them.  

Now, the standings? Yeah, we’re -1 in the AL West. But here’s the thing: the Astros are still in a rebuilding phase, and the Mariners? They’re trying to figure out how to win games. This division isn’t as stacked as it was last year. We’re not just chasing the Dodgers—we’re chasing a chance to be the team that steps into October with a pulse.  

And let’s not forget the offense. That lineup? It’s got the kind of firepower that can turn a close game into a blowout. You saw it yesterday. The Rangers scored seven runs. That’s not a fluke. That’s the kind of production that can carry you through a 162-game season. The key is consistency, and we’ve got that. We’ve got the kind of talent that can turn

pessimist.name: Buck Pierson
pessimist.column:
The Rangers lost again. Yesterday’s game was a 8-7 defeat to the Dodgers, a result that feels less like a loss and more like a slow, painful death by a thousand cuts. You’d think after seven wins and six losses, we’d have some sense of rhythm, some semblance of control. But no, the Rangers are still the same team that’s been bleeding out in October for the last decade. Pitching’s thin, the bullpen’s held together with baling wire, and the GM is on a victory tour from October. I’ve seen this movie. It ends in tears.  

Let’s talk about the pitching staff. The starters are a patchwork of guys who either got traded for a bag of chips or were signed for a cup of coffee. They’re not bad, exactly—just not good enough. You can’t rely on a starter to throw
